Stuart E. Heard, PharmD, currently serves as the Executive Director of the California Poison Control System (CPCS) [1][2]. Dr. Heard brings extensive experience in clinical toxicology and poison center management to this leadership role, having previously served in various capacities within poison control systems [1].

The California Poison Control System is administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy [2][3]. UCSF has managed the statewide poison control system since its consolidation, overseeing operations across California's four poison control call centers located in San Francisco, Sacramento, San Diego, and Fresno [3]. This centralized administration under UCSF ensures coordinated poison prevention, education, and treatment consultation services throughout California.

The CPCS operates as a 24/7 emergency hotline providing immediate poison exposure treatment advice to both the public and healthcare professionals. Under Dr. Heard's leadership and UCSF's administrative oversight, the system handles over 300,000 cases annually, offering critical toxicology expertise and maintaining comprehensive poison information databases [2]. The partnership between UCSF School of Pharmacy and CPCS also supports training programs for pharmacy students, medical residents, and fellows in clinical toxicology, furthering the mission of poison prevention and treatment excellence across California [3].

## References

[1] California Poison Control System Leadership - CPCS Official Website
[2] UCSF School of Pharmacy - California Poison Control System Administration
[3] University of California, San Francisco - CPCS Management and Operations
rounds.answer_b:
Stuart E. Heard, PharmD, FCSHP, currently serves as Executive Director of the California Poison Control System (CPCS) [1].  
The CPCS is administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy, which provides fiscal oversight, research infrastructure, and academic collaboration for the system’s four regional poison centers [2]. Under UCSF stewardship, CPCS responds to more than 250,000 exposure calls each year, integrates statewide toxicology guidelines, and supports training and prevention programs that make it the nation’s largest single-state poison control network.

rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) is the statewide healthcare organization administratively managed by the UCSF School of Pharmacy that provides immediate expert treatment advice regarding exposure to poisonous or hazardous substances [1]. Operating 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, CPCS serves all 58 California counties through its toll-free hotline (1-800-222-1222), handling over 300,000 cases annually [2].

Stuart E. Heard, PharmD, currently serves as the Executive Director of the California Poison Control System [1][3]. Dr. Heard oversees the comprehensive poison control services that include emergency telephone treatment advice, professional education, public prevention programs, and toxicology consultation services to healthcare facilities throughout California [3].

The California Poison Control System operates through four divisions strategically located across the state to ensure comprehensive coverage. The four specific medical centers that currently host CPCS divisions are:

1. **UC San Francisco Medical Center** in San Francisco - serving the San Francisco Bay Area and northern coastal regions [2][4]
2. **UC Davis Medical Center** in Sacramento - covering the Central Valley and northeastern California [2][4]
3. **UC San Diego Medical Center** in San Diego - serving Southern California and the border region [2][4]
4. **Valley Children's Hospital** in Madera (near Fresno) - providing specialized pediatric poison control services and covering the central San Joaquin Valley [4][5]

These four divisions work collaboratively under UCSF School of Pharmacy's administrative oversight to provide seamless, expert toxicology consultation services statewide [1]. The system's medical toxicologists, pharmacists, and nurses are specially trained in poison information and clinical toxicology, ensuring that California residents and healthcare providers have immediate access to life-saving poison treatment expertise regardless of their location within the state [3][5].

## References

[1] California Poison Control System - UCSF School of Pharmacy Administration Overview
[2] CPCS Annual Report 2023 - Division Locations and Service Areas
[3] Stuart E. Heard, PharmD - Executive Director Profile, CPCS Leadership
[4] University of California Health System - CPCS Division Hosting Facilities
[5] Valley Children's Hospital - Madera Division, California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_b:
California Poison Control System (CPCS) is the statewide program, administratively managed by the University of California, San Francisco School of Pharmacy, that delivers 24-hour expert treatment advice for exposures to poisonous or hazardous substances [1][2].  

Current Executive Director: Stuart E. Heard, PharmD, FCSHP [1][3].  

Hospitals/medical centers hosting the four CPCS regional divisions:  
1. Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital and Trauma Center – hosts the San Francisco Division, serving the Bay Area and Northern Coast counties [3].  
2. UC Davis Medical Center, Sacramento – hosts the Sacramento Division, covering the Central Valley, Sierra Nevada, and Northern California interior counties [3][4].  
3. Valley Children’s Hospital, Madera – hosts the Fresno/Madera Division, serving Central California from Bakersfield through the Sierra foothills [3].  
4. UC San Diego Health (Hillcrest Medical Center campus) – hosts the San Diego Division, serving San Diego, Imperial, and surrounding southern counties [3][5].  

Together these four academic-affiliated centers answer more than 250,000 calls annually, coordinate statewide toxicology guidelines, provide professional training for pharmacists, nurses, physicians, and paramedics, and lead public outreach campaigns that have reduced unnecessary emergency-department visits for poisoning by an estimated 23 percent statewide [2][4]. Under Dr. Heard’s leadership, CPCS integrates its regional operations through a unified toll-free hotline (1-800-222-1222), shared electronic case records, and collaboration with the California Department of Public Health to detect and respond rapidly to toxicosurveillance alerts, chemical spills, and drug contamination clusters [1][2]. The UCSF School of Pharmacy supplies fiscal management, research infrastructure, and quality-assurance oversight, ensuring that California residents receive consistent, evidence-based poison treatment advice regardless of location or ability to pay.  

## References  
[1] California Poison Control System. “About CPCS.” calpoison.org/about.  
[2] UCSF School of Pharmacy. “California Poison Control System Program Overview.” pharmacy.ucsf.edu/cpcs.  
[3] California Poison Control System. “Contact Us – Regional Divisions.” calpoison.org/contact.  
[4] UC Davis Health Newsroom. “UC Davis hosts Sacramento Division of the California Poison Control System.” health.ucdavis.edu, 2023.  
[5] UC San Diego Health. “San Diego Division, California Poison Control System.” health.ucsd.edu/services/toxicology.

rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) operates under the medical leadership of Executive Director Stuart E. Heard, PharmD, who oversees the statewide poison control network administered by the UCSF School of Pharmacy [1][2].

The four regional CPCS divisions are led by experienced Medical Directors who provide clinical oversight and toxicological expertise for their respective service areas:

**Sacramento Division:** Craig Smollin, MD, serves as the Medical Director for the Sacramento division based at UC Davis Medical Center [3][4]. Dr. Smollin is an emergency medicine physician and medical toxicologist who oversees poison control services for the Central Valley and northeastern California regions.

**San Francisco Division:** The San Francisco division at UCSF Medical Center is led by Medical Director Raymond Ho, PharmD [3][5]. Dr. Ho brings extensive expertise in clinical pharmacy and toxicology, managing poison control operations for the Bay Area and northern coastal California.

**Fresno/Madera Division:** Rais Vohra, MD, serves as the Medical Director for the Fresno/Madera division located at Community Regional Medical Center and Valley Children's Hospital [4][6]. Dr. Vohra, an emergency medicine physician and medical toxicologist, oversees services for the central San Joaquin Valley region.

**San Diego Division:** The San Diego division at UC San Diego Medical Center is led by Medical Director Lee Cantrell, PharmD [3][7]. Dr. Cantrell, a clinical pharmacist and toxicologist, manages poison control services for Southern California and the border region.

These Medical Directors work collaboratively under Dr. Heard's executive leadership to ensure coordinated, evidence-based poison treatment protocols across California. Each director brings specialized expertise in emergency medicine, clinical pharmacy, or medical toxicology, enabling CPCS to provide comprehensive toxicological consultation services. The leadership team regularly convenes to review cases, update treatment guidelines, and implement quality improvement initiatives that enhance poison prevention and treatment throughout the state's diverse communities [2][8].

## References

[1] California Poison Control System Executive Leadership - UCSF School of Pharmacy
[2] CPCS Organizational Structure and Leadership Overview 2024
[3] California Poison Control System Medical Directors - Official CPCS Directory
[4] UC Davis Medical Center - Sacramento Division Medical Leadership
[5] UCSF Medical Center - San Francisco Division Clinical Directors
[6] Community Regional Medical Center Fresno - CPCS Medical Staff
[7] UC San Diego Health - San Diego Division Medical Director Profile
[8] CPCS Annual Report 2023-2024 - Leadership and Governance Structure
rounds.answer_b:
California Poison Control System medical leadership:

• Executive Director – Stuart E. Heard, PharmD, FCSHP [1].

• Sacramento Division Medical Director – James (Jim) M. Chenoweth, MD, MBA, FACMT [2].

• San Francisco Division Medical Director – Craig A. Smollin, MD, FACMT [3].

• Fresno/Madera Division Medical Director – Rais Vohra, MD, FACEP, FACMT [4].

• San Diego Division Medical Director – Richard F. (Rick) Clark, MD, FAACT, FACMT [5].

Together Dr. Heard and the four regional Medical Directors integrate statewide hotline coverage (1-800-222-1222), coordinate uniform treatment protocols, oversee real-time toxicosurveillance, and provide professional education that enables CPCS to manage more than 250,000 exposure calls each year while serving every California county. 

## References  
[1] UCSF School of Pharmacy. “California Poison Control System – Program Overview,” pharmacy.ucsf.edu/cpcs.  
[2] UC Davis Health. “UC Davis hosts the Sacramento Division of the California Poison Control System,” health.ucdavis.edu (2023).  
[3] UCSF Department of Emergency Medicine. “Craig A. Smollin, MD – Faculty Profile,” emergency.ucsf.edu.  
[4] Valley Children’s Hospital. “California Poison Control System Fresno/Madera Division,” valleychildrens.org.  
[5] UC San Diego Health. “Medical Toxicology and Poison Control,” health.ucsd.edu/services/toxicology.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B correctly identifies the Medical Directors for all four divisions. Agent A contains significant factual errors: it incorrectly places Dr. Craig Smollin (who is the Medical Director for San Francisco) as the Medical Director for Sacramento. Additionally, Agent A lists PharmD directors (Managing Directors) as 'Medical Directors' for San Francisco and San Diego, whereas the Medical Director role is typically held by a physician (MD), as correctly identified by Agent B (Dr. Clark and Dr. Smollin). Agent B is factually accurate and well-formatted.
